Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

George Croom Robertson

1842 – 1892

The first editor of Mind: philosophy of mind as an emerging scientific discipline.

The most compact reconstruction is this: George Croom Robertson’s philosophy of mind is a late-Victorian, empiricist, developmental, physiologically informed, non-reductive philosophy of subjective experience. He treats mind first as “subjective experience”; he treats psychology as a positive natural science of that experience; he insists that every mental process has a bodily or nervous concomitant; but he refuses to reduce psychology either to physiology or to metaphysical materialism. Philosophy of mind, for him, begins in psychology but does not end there: psychology explains the natural genesis, conditions, and laws of mental life, while philosophy asks about the validity of knowledge, the import of objectivity, and the ultimate meaning of subject, object, reality, and being.

1. Textual basis and interpretive caution

Robertson did not leave a single finished systematic treatise called “philosophy of mind.” The main sources for reconstructing his view are his article “Psychology and Philosophy” in Mind in 1883, the posthumous Elements of Psychology, and the posthumous Elements of General Philosophy. The two Elements volumes were edited by C. A. Foley Rhys Davids from lecture notes delivered at University College London between 1870 and 1892, supplemented by Robertson’s own manuscripts and by student notebooks; this makes them indispensable but not identical in authority to an author-prepared monograph.

That limitation matters because Robertson’s thought is partly a methodological project rather than a closed metaphysical system. He is best read as trying to secure the scientific standing of psychology while also preventing psychology from absorbing the whole of philosophy. His own journal, Mind, was founded in 1876 as a platform for the professionalization of English-language work in psychology and philosophy; the journal’s present historical summary explicitly notes Robertson’s concern with whether psychology could pass into the company of the sciences.

2. Historical setting: why Robertson matters

Robertson stands at a transitional moment in the history of philosophy of mind. Before the sharp twentieth-century institutional separation between psychology and philosophy, “mind” was still the common territory of epistemology, logic, metaphysics, moral psychology, physiological psychology, and associationist psychology. Mind was founded precisely in this unstable territory. Christopher Green’s historical study notes that the early journal aimed to determine whether scientific psychology was viable and where its boundaries lay with philosophy and with older discredited “scientific” studies of mind such as phrenology and mesmerism; by the mid-1880s it even became, for a time, a leading English-language venue for experimental psychology.

Recent scholarship on early Mind places Robertson with Alexander Bain as part of an inductivist project: philosophy was to be made scientific by being placed on the basis of empirical psychology, especially associationist psychology. But this same scholarship also warns against reducing early Mind to a mere “home of associationism,” because the journal also engaged Kantian and idealist questions about synthesis, unity of mind, selfhood, neurophysiology, and materialism. Robertson’s own view is therefore best located between British empiricism, associationism, physiological psychology, and post-Kantian concern with the constitutive activity of mind.

3. Psychology as the positive science of mind

Robertson’s first major thesis is methodological: psychology is the science of mind, but philosophy is not reducible to psychology. In Elements of Psychology, he begins by distinguishing “philosophy of mind” from the “science of psychology.” Psychology is the reasoned, systematic science of mind; philosophy has a wider task because it concerns the ultimate notions that underlie the sciences.

His definition of mind is explicitly subjective. Mind is not first a substance, a soul, a hidden entity, or a faculty-machine; it is “subjective experience,” the experience each person has for himself or herself. This is why psychology differs from the objective sciences. The physical sciences consider things in their objective relations; psychology considers experience as owned, lived, and subjectively given. The “subject” is initially the possessor of this special experience, not yet a metaphysical soul-substance.

Robertson also insists that psychology is a natural science, but he means “natural” in method, not material in subject matter. Psychology is natural because it must proceed by observation, analysis, comparison, hypothesis, and law-seeking explanation; it is not natural because mental facts are merely physical facts. He is careful to say that mind is observed first in one’s own experience, and that objective observation of others must be interpreted through subjective observation.

This gives him a dual evidential standpoint. Subjective psychology is primary because the meaning of mental terms is rooted in first-person experience. Objective psychology is indispensable but supplementary: behavior, expression, language, social practices, animal action, and physiological process all provide evidence for mind, but they are interpreted as manifestations of subjective experience. He explicitly says that we infer other minds and even animal pain from behavior, while treating subjective experience as the ultimate psychological datum.

4. The basic ontology of mind: phenomenal, not substantival

Robertson’s psychology begins phenomenally. To do science, he says, one must treat the subject phenomenally; the psychologist therefore starts with mental phenomena rather than with a metaphysical doctrine of the ego, soul, or substance. This is not a denial that there may be a deeper bearer of experience; it is a methodological suspension. In the psychological science of mind, one begins from the given flow of experience and only later raises the philosophical problem of what bears or unifies that experience.

This is one of the most important points for extracting his philosophy of mind. Robertson is not a Cartesian dualist, because he does not begin with a thinking substance. He is not a reductive materialist, because subjective experience remains an irreducible datum for psychology. He is not a pure behaviorist, because behavior is evidence of mind, not the whole meaning of mind. He is not a transcendental idealist in the strict Kantian sense, because he expects psychology to give empirical and developmental accounts of mental synthesis. His position is best described as methodological phenomenalism plus psychophysical naturalism.

The term “inner” is also treated cautiously. Robertson notes that psychological language is often metaphorical; “inner” and “internal” are spatial metaphors that can mislead if taken literally. “Conscious experience” is a less metaphorical expression because it does not picture mind as a small thing inside the body.

5. Consciousness: continuity, analysis, and the field of mind

Robertson treats mental life as continuous before it is discrete. Consciousness is not naturally given as an inventory of separate faculties or atomized states; it is a flow or continuity. Scientific psychology must analyze this flow, but the analysis is an abstraction. The final account must return to the continuity from which it began. Psychology “is worthless,” in effect, if its analysis destroys the living unity of consciousness.

This leads him to reject old-style faculty psychology as explanatory. Faculty terms may be descriptively convenient, but they do not explain mental life. To say that memory remembers, will wills, or reason reasons is not a scientific explanation. What psychology must explain is how mental processes arise, differentiate, combine, and develop within the continuous stream of subjective experience.

Yet Robertson does not collapse everything into an undifferentiated stream. He divides mental life into three “phases”: feeling, intellection, and conation. He prefers “intellection” to “cognition” because “knowledge” already carries philosophical implications about the object known. He prefers “conation” to “will” because will is too narrow: conation includes the whole active or striving side of mind, from impulse and desire to fully developed volition.

The tripartition is not a partition into three separate mental substances or faculties. Feeling, intellection, and conation are aspects or phases of a single mental life. In concrete cases they interpenetrate: feeling gives subjective value, intellection supplies discrimination and representation, and conation supplies tendency, activity, striving, or action.

6. Feeling: subjective affection, pleasure, pain, and emotion

Feeling is Robertson’s name for the passive-affective side of consciousness: being affected. In its narrower psychological sense, feeling is experience in the forms of pleasure and pain, though he is aware of broader uses of the term in common speech and in Spencer. Feeling is not merely a low-grade sensation; it is a pervasive phase of mental life because all experience can have a subjective tone of elevation, depression, pleasure, pain, interest, or aversion.

Emotion is not simply raw sensation. Robertson describes emotion as centrally initiated feeling, often involving representation and always bearing a close relation to bodily expression. In this respect he belongs to the nineteenth-century tradition that takes Darwin, Bain, and Spencer seriously on expression, bodily manifestation, and the physiology of feeling, but he preserves the subjective dimension: emotion is a state of the subject as a whole, not merely a muscular event.

The upshot is that affect is not secondary ornamentation added to cognition. Feeling is one of the poles of consciousness. It motivates conation, colors perception, and contributes to attention and value. Robertson’s philosophy of mind is therefore not a purely intellectualist theory of mind; it is a theory in which cognition, affect, and action are structurally interdependent.

7. Conation, will, attention, and action

Conation is the active pole of consciousness. Robertson uses it for “tending to act,” covering desire, impulse, striving, and will. Conation is always related to feeling: action is guided by felt want, uneasiness, interest, pleasure, pain, or the anticipation of such states. The end of action, in the first or last instance, is expressible in terms of feeling: the production, maintenance, or removal of some affective condition.

Will is a developed form of conation, not an original faculty. Robertson follows Bain in treating will as something that develops in the individual through experience, bodily control, imitation, and social life. Voluntary action is purposive action: action directed toward an end represented by the agent, together with means toward that end. In mature will, the represented end may drop into the background, but voluntary action still depends on a prior representation of ends.

This developmental account is important. It rejects the older idea that the will is an inexplicable primitive power. It also avoids reducing action to reflex. Human will grows out of sensori-motor and ideo-motor processes, but it surpasses them when represented ends, personal purposes, and the empirical ego enter into action.

Attention is the bridge between conation and intellection. Robertson calls attention a kind of covert activity: intellection voluntarily put forth. It can be involuntary or voluntary, and there is no hard boundary between the two. Voluntary attention makes an object clearer as a whole and more distinct in its parts; in perception it involves muscular activity, and in thought it involves a motor attitude even when no overt movement is obvious.

This produces a very modern-sounding view: attention is not merely a spotlight passively illuminating ideas; it is an active, bodily conditioned, interest-guided reorganization of consciousness. Scientific thought and artistic imagination are, for Robertson, “constructive” activities of the volitional type, because they represent ends under the impulse of strong emotional interest.

8. Intellection: sensation, perception, image, concept, thought

Robertson’s account of knowing is empiricist, but not passively sensationalist. Sensations are not self-sufficient building blocks that automatically copy the world. Percepts are intellectual constructions out of sensory material. Psychology must explain the laws according to which these constructions come to pass.

Perception is especially important because it is where Robertson’s empiricism becomes active and motor-based. Objective perception depends on activity put forth and resisted. Passive sensation alone does not yield objectivity. The “heart” of object-quality is not mere color, sound, or passive feel, but resistance encountered through active touch and muscular sense. He treats objective perception as beginning from active touch and muscular activity, with the object first given psychologically as resistance.

His theory of space follows the same active pattern. Genetically, we apprehend body as resisting before we apprehend space as empty extension. Consciousness of space arises from touch and activity together as co-factors. This gives Robertson a motor theory of spatial perception: space is not simply read off from passive sensation, nor is it simply an a priori form in Kant’s sense; it is constructed through embodied activity.

He also gives a constructive account of imagination and perception. The image is a reproduced percept without present sense-stimulation. Hallucination and illusion reveal how much imagination enters into perception. One striking formulation is that perception is verifiable imagination on the occasion of sense-stimulation, whereas illusion is unverifiable.

Concepts are higher-order representative products. Percepts and images are singular; concepts are general. The concept brings together a multiplicity of images on the ground of similarity, producing a unity out of plurality. In perception, discrimination is dominant; in conception, assimilation is dominant.

Association remains central but is not treated crudely. Robertson accepts similarity and contiguity as the two fundamental principles governing the flow of representative consciousness. He is historically conscious of Aristotle, Hobbes, Locke, Hume, Hartley, the Mills, Bain, Spencer, Wundt, and others, and he treats association as a law of mental synthesis rather than as a mere mechanical linkage of atomized ideas.

9. Thought, language, and social mind

Robertson’s philosophy of mind is not individualistic in the older empiricist sense. He holds that developed thought depends deeply on language, and that language is social in origin. Perception and imagination can occur without speech, but abstract concepts probably require language as a system of signs. Speech arises primarily as communication between persons, not merely as a private label attached by an isolated mind to its own ideas.

This is one of the points at which he moves beyond simple associationism. Thought is not just a private succession of ideas; developed thought is socially scaffolded by language. Robertson says thought is social in origin and only later becomes individual. Once language exists, it educates and extends thought, and even developed perception is improved by naming.

This means Robertson’s theory contains a proto-social philosophy of mind. The individual mind is not denied, but higher cognition is formed through communication, expression, imitation, and shared signs. This also strengthens his rejection of a purely private Cartesian mind: the mature mind is not an isolated inner substance but a subject whose thinking has been shaped by social expression.

10. The ego: empirical self, not metaphysical soul

Robertson treats the ego with special caution. The question “what experiences the experiences?” is, for him, profound but not immediately answerable within elementary psychology. In psychology, mind must first be treated as an aggregate or flow of phenomena; the bearer of those phenomena is a problem to be worked up to, and ultimately belongs to philosophy.

Still, he does not ignore the self. He distinguishes the empirical ego from the pure ego. The only ego psychology can use is an ego that has developed gradually: a deposit or accretion of life-experience, character, purposes, ideals, and aspirations. The self is not an innate metaphysical substance available to psychology as an explanatory primitive. It is a developed structure within experience.

In mature volition, the ego becomes a direct factor. Actions are not fully explained by links between particular ideas and feelings once the person has developed self-respect, personal ends, and a persistent sense of self. This is why Robertson says the free-will question has no meaning unless action is considered in relation to a subject who acts.

So the reconstructed view is: the metaphysical subject is suspended; the empirical self is developed; mature agency requires self-reference; and psychology explains selfhood in terms of experience, representation, attention, character, and purpose. That is a sophisticated middle position between Humean bundle theory and Cartesian ego-substance.

11. Mind and body: psychophysical concomitance, not reduction

Robertson’s mind-body view is one of psychophysical concomitance or parallelism, with an important asymmetry. With every psychosis there is a concomitant neurosis, but not every neurosis has a psychosis. In simpler terms: every mental event has a nervous correlate, but not every nervous event is conscious or mental. The nervous system does much work besides mind, but mind always involves nervous activity.

This is not Cartesian interactionism. Robertson does not posit an immaterial soul pushing the body. Nor is it crude identity theory. The psychological fact and the physiological fact are correlated, but psychology retains its own subject matter because subjective experience cannot be replaced by descriptions of nerve-processes.

He interprets much action as reflex or automatic, but not all action is thereby reduced to reflex. Some actions now automatic were once conscious; habit can transform conscious action into automatic process. Conscious neurosis proceeds by way of the brain, and mental life must be understood in relation to the nervous system, but psychology must still describe the subjective side.

Robertson also allows for degrees of consciousness and for latent or unconscious mental modifications. His position is cautious: consciousness is not simply either fully present or absent. It has foreground and background, degrees of intensity, marginal states, and possibly unconscious subjective conditions standing in definite relation to nervous process. By the end of the appendix on latent mental modifications, he says his thought tends toward the assumption that mind, understood as subjective experience, and fully conscious awareness are not coextensive.

The resulting philosophy of mind is non-reductive but embodied: mental life is subjective, continuous, and first-personally given; nevertheless, every mental process is biologically and neurologically conditioned.

12. Psychology and philosophy: genesis versus validity

Robertson’s 1883 article “Psychology and Philosophy” is central to his overall position. He explicitly warns against confusing psychology with philosophy. Terms such as “mental philosophy” and “philosophy of mind” can obscure the distinction. Psychology is a positive phenomenal science; philosophy asks questions that psychology alone cannot answer.

The distinction is clearest in the case of knowledge. Psychology studies knowing as a phase of conscious experience: how it arises, how it is naturally conditioned, how it develops from sense, perception, representation, association, thought, language, and action. Philosophy asks about the import, validity, and objective meaning of knowledge. The psychologist explains perceiving as a fact of experience; the philosopher asks what perceiving imports about an independent object or reality.

This is why Robertson is not a simple psychologistic reductionist. Psychology supplies data and method to philosophy, but it does not settle philosophical normativity by itself. Logic, for example, is not merely a description of how people happen to think; it concerns the validity of thought in general. Likewise, epistemology asks about knowledge as knowledge, not merely about the natural history of believing.

Nevertheless, psychology has philosophical primacy in method. All objects and facts can be considered as conscious experience, and in that sense psychology’s scope is as wide as philosophy’s scope, though its task is different. Psychology gives philosophy the disciplined account of mind without which epistemology and metaphysics become dogmatic.

Robertson’s mature ordering is: first psychology as positive science of mental phenomena; then theory of knowledge; then, only after that, metaphysics or ontology. He rejects dogmatic assertion of Being at the outset. Philosophy may still render an account of concrete realities and ultimate meaning, but it must first determine the modes and validity of knowing.

13. The soul and incorporeal minds

Robertson does not appear to offer a positive doctrine of an immortal soul or an incorporeal mind as part of his scientific psychology. In Elements of Psychology, the deepest question of the bearer of experience is explicitly postponed; psychology begins with subjective experience and its phenomena, not with the metaphysical constitution of the experiencer.

When he discusses “soul” in Elements of General Philosophy, he usually does so historically. He traces pre-philosophical animism, in which soul is imagined as a subtle or ethereal body within the body and then projected onto stones, rivers, and other things. He treats this as a stage prior to a scientific conception of mind as subjective.

His treatment of Greek philosophy is especially revealing. He sees Aristotle as moving toward the modern subjective-objective distinction by refusing to treat mind simply as body or as something merely apart from body. Robertson says Aristotle did not state subjectivity as the characteristic of mind, but implied it. This is important because Robertson’s own view similarly refuses both crude materialism and crude separable-soul dualism.

His treatment of Descartes is more critical. He presents Cartesian philosophy as a dogmatic dualism between thinking substance and extended substance. Descartes begins from reflective self-consciousness but then moves, in Robertson’s account, into the dogmatic thesis that the self is a thinking substance opposed to non-thinking extended substance.

Robertson is especially critical of the Cartesian treatment of animals as automata without soul or mind. He notes that Descartes’ view that animals have life but not mind is not borne out by observation and is no longer maintained except incautiously. He also emphasizes that Descartes’ own mind-body dualism runs into difficulty because facts testify to a relation between mind and body.

On Spinoza and Leibniz, Robertson’s interest is again historical and methodological. He sees Spinoza as an early source of parallelism and notes that modern parallelism can be used phenomenally for psychological science without deciding ultimate metaphysical hypotheses. He treats Leibniz as an attempt to unite mechanism and teleology through the monad, where soul and body are not two separate beings but two primordial forces or aspects.

The safest conclusion is this: Robertson’s own philosophy of mind does not need an incorporeal soul as an explanatory posit. The scientific account of mind begins with subjective experience, empirical ego-development, embodied action, and psychophysical concomitance. Questions about ultimate substance, immortality, or incorporeal mind are not denied outright as meaningless, but they are removed from psychology and placed under later philosophical inquiry. His view is therefore anti-animistic, anti-Cartesian in method, and non-committal about soul-substance except as a historical and metaphysical problem.

14. Comprehensive extraction: Robertson’s philosophy of mind in theses

Robertson’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed in the following theses.

First, mind is subjective experience. The primary datum of psychology is not behavior, brain, soul-substance, or abstract self, but lived experience as possessed by a subject. This is why psychology has a distinctive place among the sciences.

Second, psychology is a natural science by method. It must be empirical, analytic, comparative, developmental, and law-seeking. But its object is sui generis: subjective experience cannot be simply replaced by physical description.

Third, introspection is indispensable but insufficient by itself. First-person observation gives the meaning of mental life; objective observation, behavior, expression, social artifacts, language, comparative psychology, and physiology provide controls and extensions.

Fourth, consciousness is continuous. Mental states are scientific abstractions from a living flow. Psychology may analyze, but it must return synthetically to the continuity of mind.

Fifth, mental life has three irreducible phases: feeling, intellection, and conation. Feeling is subjective affection; intellection is discriminative and assimilative knowing; conation is tendency toward action. They are phases of one process, not separate faculties.

Sixth, perception is active construction. Objective perception arises through sensation, muscular activity, resistance, representation, and verification. The object is not passively copied; it is constituted psychologically through active engagement with resistance and space.

Seventh, association and suggestion are laws of representative consciousness. Similarity and contiguity govern the flow of images, memories, concepts, and trains of thought, but they operate within an active, continuous, embodied consciousness rather than in a merely mechanical idea-machine.

Eighth, thought is socially formed through language. Rudimentary perception and imagination can exist without speech, but developed abstract thought depends on signs, communication, and shared language. Thought is social in origin before it becomes private in use.

Ninth, the self is empirical before it is metaphysical. Psychology can explain the empirical ego as a developmental accretion of experience, character, purposes, ideals, and attention. The “pure ego” or ultimate bearer is a philosophical problem, not a psychological starting point.

Tenth, will is developed purposive action. Volition grows out of sensori-motor and ideo-motor life, but mature will involves represented ends, self-reference, social formation, attention, and personal purposes.

Eleventh, every mental process has a nervous concomitant, but the correlation is asymmetrical. Every psychosis has a neurosis; not every neurosis has a psychosis. This gives Robertson a psychophysical parallelism or concomitance without reduction to materialism.

Twelfth, mind may outrun focal consciousness. Robertson accepts degrees of consciousness, marginal states, and possibly latent mental modifications; he tends toward the view that mind and fully conscious awareness are not coextensive.

Thirteenth, philosophy of mind depends on psychology but is not psychology. Psychology explains mental processes as natural facts; philosophy asks about validity, objectivity, truth, being, and ultimate interpretation.

Fourteenth, soul-substance is not a scientific explanatory primitive. Soul-language belongs mainly to historical metaphysics, animism, Platonism, Aristotelian psychology, Cartesian dualism, Spinozistic parallelism, and Leibnizian monadology. Robertson’s own scientific account starts instead from subjective experience, empirical selfhood, embodiment, and psychophysical concomitance.

15. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Robertson is a hinge figure. Looking backward, he inherits Locke, Hume, Hartley, the Mills, Bain, and British associationism. Like them, he tries to explain complex mental life from experience, association, representation, and development. But he is not merely repeating eighteenth-century associationism. He adds physiological psychology, psychophysical concomitance, active perception, motor theories of space, attention, empirical ego-development, language, and social formation.

Looking sideways, he is in dialogue with German and post-Kantian philosophy. He does not accept a purely transcendental account of the forms of experience, but he takes seriously the problem of synthesis: how objects, space, concepts, and selfhood are actively constituted. His own psychology of space and object perception tries to give empirical warrant to issues that Kant had handled transcendentally. In “Psychology and Philosophy,” Robertson explicitly suggests that modern psychology of space, active synthesis, and muscular movement can yield philosophically important results concerning Kantian themes.

Looking forward, Robertson anticipates several later developments. His continuous consciousness resembles what William James would famously call the stream of consciousness, though Robertson remains more closely tied to British associationist and Bainian frameworks. His conative and functional account of will anticipates functionalist concerns with action and adaptation. His treatment of perception as active, motor-involving, and constructive anticipates later embodied and enactive themes, though without their contemporary vocabulary. His distinction between psychology and validity-questions anticipates the later anti-psychologistic separation between empirical psychology and logic or epistemology.

His historical importance is therefore not that he produced a famous doctrine comparable to Cartesian dualism, Hume’s bundle theory, or James’s radical empiricism. It is that he occupies the moment when philosophy of mind, psychology, physiology, epistemology, and logic were being renegotiated into their modern institutional forms. He represents a serious attempt to make philosophy of mind scientifically responsible without surrendering the irreducibility of subjective experience or the autonomy of philosophical questions.

16. Final assessment

Robertson’s philosophy of mind is best named a non-reductive empirical philosophy of subjective experience. It is empirical because all psychological explanation begins from experience and its development. It is subjective because mind is first-personal experience, not merely behavior or nerve-process. It is embodied because every mental process has a nervous concomitant and because perception, attention, emotion, and will are inseparable from activity and expression. It is developmental because mind, will, self, language, and thought grow through experience, imitation, social life, and representation. It is non-reductive because physiology and psychology are correlated but not identical. It is anti-dogmatic because metaphysics must come after psychology and theory of knowledge, not before them.

On the soul, the conclusion is negative but philosophically meaningful: Robertson does not build his view around an incorporeal mind. The “soul” is treated chiefly as a historical concept, and the metaphysical bearer of experience is postponed beyond psychology. His science of mind needs no Cartesian soul, but neither does it collapse mind into matter. It leaves us with a disciplined picture of mind as lived, active, embodied, social, continuous, and philosophically inexhaustible.
